coredata.dc:title: Li-ion battery charge transfer stability studies with direct current impedance spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Selected papers from 2022 7th International Conference on New Energy and Future Energy Systems
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Yun
Gong, Yinchu
coredata.dc:description:
The safety of lithium-ion batteries is closely related to the charge transfer process inside the battery. With the rapid increase in battery usage, battery safety issues have become increasingly prominent. Due to technical limitations, the current battery thermal failure management system cannot realize risk early warning. Charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) is a macroscopic parameter characterizing the battery transport process, which can be detected by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Due to the high cost of EIS test equipment and high signal-to-noise ratio requirements, it is still not applicable to electric vehicles. In addition,
R
c t
is affected by ambient temperature and battery aging, which brings challenges to online safety diagnosis. Here we propose a method to obtain the activation energy of a battery using direct current impedance spectroscopy (DCIS), which enables the stability diagnosis of the charge transport process. DCIS is a time-domain impedance spectroscopy technique. It uses the time constant characteristic of the internal resistor–capacitor network to detect battery parameters. Activation energy is the energy barrier for lithium-ions to cross the electrode/electrolyte interface and can indicate the stability of the charge transport process. Since activation energy is a microscopic parameter, it is not affected by temperature and aging, which is beneficial for battery safety diagnosis. The experimental results show that the charge transfer process of the sample cells with a state of health (SOH) greater than 80% is stable. When the SOH is less than 80%, the activation energy drops rapidly from 0.55 eV to 0.16 eV, indicating an increased risk of thermal runaway. Since DCIS supports online detection, it provides an option for safety diagnosis of electric vehicle batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Direct current impedance spectroscopy
Activation energy
Charge transfer resistance
Safety diagnosis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery structure and equivalent circuit.
Schematic diagram of (a) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; (b) direct current impedance spectroscopy.
Multi-temperature DCIS of aged battery samples.

Variation of
R
c t
with temperature.
Internal resistance (IR), capacity and SOH of the battery samples. (Room temperature).
Charge transfer resistance of battery samples with temperature.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: The 7th International Conference on New Energy and Future Energy Systems (NEFES 2022), 7th NEFES, 25–28 October 2022, Nanjing (virtually), China

head.ce:title: Li-ion battery charge transfer stability studies with direct current impedance spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, Donghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The safety of lithium-ion batteries is closely related to the charge transfer process inside the battery. With the rapid increase in battery usage, battery safety issues have become increasingly prominent. Due to technical limitations, the current battery thermal failure management system cannot realize risk early warning. Charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) is a macroscopic parameter characterizing the battery transport process, which can be detected by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Due to the high cost of EIS test equipment and high signal-to-noise ratio requirements, it is still not applicable to electric vehicles. In addition,
R
c t
is affected by ambient temperature and battery aging, which brings challenges to online safety diagnosis. Here we propose a method to obtain the activation energy of a battery using direct current impedance spectroscopy (DCIS), which enables the stability diagnosis of the charge transport process. DCIS is a time-domain impedance spectroscopy technique. It uses the time constant characteristic of the internal resistor–capacitor network to detect battery parameters. Activation energy is the energy barrier for lithium-ions to cross the electrode/electrolyte interface and can indicate the stability of the charge transport process. Since activation energy is a microscopic parameter, it is not affected by temperature and aging, which is beneficial for battery safety diagnosis. The experimental results show that the charge transfer process of the sample cells with a state of health (SOH) greater than 80% is stable. When the SOH is less than 80%, the activation energy drops rapidly from 0.55 eV to 0.16 eV, indicating an increased risk of thermal runaway. Since DCIS supports online detection, it provides an option for safety diagnosis of electric vehicle batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in electric vehicles and energy storage systems due to their efficient energy storage properties. However, with the rapid development of the power lithium-ion battery market, the safety of lithium-ion batteries has received extensive attention. Any lithium-ion battery fire incident will trigger a large number of lithium-ion batteries to be called, causing serious economic damage and reputational problems to the business. Although battery manufacturers can improve the reliability of cells through process improvement, the absolute reliability of probability cannot be achieved. In addition, the power lithium-ion battery pack is composed of hundreds of battery cells, which also magnifies the possibility of system failure. For example, when the battery failure rate is 0.1 ppm, there will be one failed car in 10,000 T Model S [1]. Therefore, the safety of li-ion battery has become a problem that restricts the rapid development of electric vehicles and needs to be solved urgently [2].

Internal failures of Li-ion batteries are caused by the instability of the electrochemical system, and thermal runaway caused by internal failures can lead to detrimental battery safety risks [3,4]. Side reactions between electrodes and electrolytes are one of the main reasons for internal failures [5,6], which can cause electrolyte decomposition, heat build-up, release of oxygen, and damage to the separator. Recently, some researchers have begun to try to solve this problem from the perspective of material properties, and have made some useful progress [7,8]. Electrochemistry uses charge transfer resistance (
R
c t
) to represent the charge transport process of lithium-ions between electrodes and electrolyte, which can be tested by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [9–11]. EIS is an alternating current (AC) impedance spectroscopy method that uses signals of different frequencies as trigger sources. Since different structures inside the battery have different frequency characteristics, EIS can obtain the internal structure parameters of the battery, such as
R
c t
, through frequency domain measurement. Due to expensive hardware, high signal-to-noise ratio requirements, and difficult dynamic detection, this technology has not yet achieved commercial application in electric vehicles [12]. In addition,
R
c t
is not a fixed value, it will change with battery temperature and aging. These changes are unavoidable for battery packs of electric vehicles. Therefore, using
R
c t
for battery safety diagnosis needs to consider the difficulties caused by temperature changes and aging.

In this paper, we propose a method to detect the activation energy of the electrode/electrolyte interface using the direct current impedance spectroscopy (DCIS) technique. This method enables the diagnosis of charge transport stability inside the battery, indicating battery safety risks. Different from the EIS method, DCIS obtains parameters through the time constant characteristics of the internal structure of the battery, and is an impedance spectroscopy technique suitable for on-line detection of battery packs. Activation energy is the energy barrier that lithium-ions need to overcome when passing through the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte, and is an important indicator of the lithium-ion transport capacity at the interface [13–16]. As a microscopic parameter, the activation energy is not related to the battery temperature and aging, and can well reflect the stability of the interface. In the experiment, we measured
R
c t
at different temperatures by DCIS, and then used the Arrhenius equation to obtain the activation energy of the interface. This method can not only realize online diagnosis, but also avoid the influence of external temperature and internal aging of the battery, which is a suitable method for the safety diagnosis of electric vehicle batteries.

During the initial cycle charge and discharge of lithium batteries, some lithium-ions will react with non-aqueous solvents to form a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film on the electrode surface, preventing the continued decomposition of the electrolyte in the carbon electrode [17–19]. Lithium-ions exist in the electrolyte as solvated particles. They need to pass through the SEI to reach the electrodes, where intercalation/de-intercalation reactions occur. As shown in Fig. 1, the charge transport between the electrode and the electrolyte consists of three parts, including (1) the solvation/de-solvation process of Li-ions at the SEI/electrolyte interface (2) the Li-ion transport process in the SEI (3) The intercalation/de-intercalation process of lithium-ions at the SEI/electrode interface. The electrochemical model represents the electrode/electrolyte interface with a resistive–capacitive (RC) network composed of
R
c t
and double-layer capacitance (
C
d l
). Here
R
c t
is the resistance generated by the charge transfer process, which reflects the transport properties of lithium-ions between electrodes/electrolytes.

Due to the frequency characteristics of the RC network [20,21], the impedance value of the lithium-ion battery can be expressed as
(1)
Z
ω
=
R
0
+
1
j ω
C
d l
+
1
R
c t
=
R
0
+
R
c t
j ω τ + 1

Here
R
0
is the ohmic resistance, ω is the RC test signal frequency, and τ is the time constant of the RC network. EIS can obtain the impedance value of the battery in the frequency domain space through multi-frequency signal measurement. The Nyquist plot in Fig. 2a is a frequency domain space EIS curve [22–24]. As the frequency decreases, a semicircular impedance curve caused by the
R
c t
/
C
d l
network appears in the upper half of the Nyquist plot, the diameter of which is equal to
R
c t
. The frequency at the vertex of the semicircle corresponds to the time constant τ of the
R
c t
/
C
d l
network [25,26].

Different from the EIS technique in the frequency domain, DCIS is a time-domain impedance spectroscopy technique. The principle is to use the time constant characteristics of the RC network. When the width time of the pulse discharge signal is close to the time constant τ of the RC network, the output voltage of the RC network will show a strong time-exponential curve characteristic. According to this time function, the parameter values of the RC network can be directly measured in the time domain. For example, the DC pulse discharge current is I, and a voltage change of Δ V occurs across the battery. Due to the RC network, the equivalent resistance of the battery is a function of time and can be expressed as
(2)
R
¯
t
=
Δ V
t
I
=
R
0
+
R
c t
( 1 −
e
−
t
τ
)

Here τ is the time constant of the RC network. By changing the discharge pulse width, DCIS can obtain
R
c t
in the time domain using the time function of the equivalent resistance, as shown in Fig. 2b. We have proved in our previous research that
R
¯
( t )
and impedance Z(
ω
) can be converted to each other through Fourier transform [27]. Due to the low cost and high signal-to-noise ratio of DC diagnostics, DCIS is a suitable technology for online impedance detection of electric vehicle battery packs.

Since the working environment temperature and aging parameters of power cells are uncertain, these will change the value of
R
c t
. Therefore, there are issues of temperature and aging when using
R
c t
to study the stability of the charge transport process. As the charge transport process of lithium-ions is related to its diffusivity, the diffusion coefficient follows the Arrhenius equation [28,29],
(3)
1
R
c t
= A e x p
( −
E
a
R T
)

Here A is the proportionality coefficient,
E
a
is the activation energy, R is the gas constant, and T is the Kelvin temperature. Activation energy is an important indicator of the diffusivity of lithium-ions, and the diffusion coefficient decays exponentially with the increase of the energy barrier. In addition, as a microscopic parameter, the energy barrier of the electrode/electrolyte interface is not affected by temperature, which is beneficial for battery safety diagnosis in variable temperature environments.

The experiment used the same batch of commercial 18650 lithium-ion batteries as the investigation samples. Experimental samples with different aging degrees were obtained by multiple constant current and constant voltage (CC-CV) charging and constant current (CC) discharging cycles. As the aging degree of the battery increases, the maximum capacity of the battery decreases, while the internal resistance increases. The room temperature internal resistance, capacity and SOH of the experimental battery samples are shown in Table 1. The internal resistance here is the traditional DC internal resistance, which is composed of multiple impedances such as electrolyte resistance and charge transfer resistance.

In the experiment, we measured the
R
c t
value of the battery samples by the DCIS method under different temperature environments. The pulse discharge current is 0.25 A, the width is gradually increased from 10 ms to 500 ms, and the step is 10 ms. The interval between two measurements is 10 s to ensure that the battery has sufficient recovery time. The sample cells are all at 100% state of charge, which facilitates the detection of the transport state of the SEI interface at the anode. The same sample cell was tested at multiple temperatures, including − 10 °C, 0 °C, 10 °C, 30 °C and 50 °C. Fig. 3 shows the DCIS of each sample cell. For the same cell, the DCIS curves are significantly different as the temperature changes, which indicates the
R
c t
temperature characteristics. As the temperature increases, the change in the DCIS curve becomes smaller, which means that the
R
c t
value decreases. The
R
c t
of battery samples at different temperatures obtained by DCIS are shown in Table 2. The reason for the decrease in
R
c t
is that the diffusivity of lithium-ions increases with temperature. In this work, we used a simplified first-order RC network, and also used a simplified plate capacitor model. Compared with the
R
c t
obtained by EIS using the constant phase element (CPE) model [11], the value is a bit large. Since what we consider here is the transport process of lithium ions across the entire interface, there is no need to refine the impedance of each part, and this result is acceptable.

Further research shows that there is an exponential relationship between
R
c t
and temperature, as shown in Fig. 4. This result is consistent with the Arrhenius equation. The activation energy of the battery sample can be obtained by exponential fitting. In the experiment, the activation energy of samples 1–4 is basically around 0.55 eV, which is consistent with the activation energy range (0.31–0.94 eV) of the 18650 battery reported in the literature [30]. At the same time, within the normal aging range, the activation energy of the battery is basically stable, which is consistent with the conclusion obtained in literature [31]. The activation energies of samples 5 and 6 are only 0.16 eV and 0.18 eV, respectively. Comparing these activation energies with SOH, the reason for the rapid drop in values can be found. As shown in Fig. 5, the SOH of samples 1–4 are all greater than 80%, which is a reasonable and healthy working state of lithium-ion batteries. The SOH of both samples 5 and 6 is less than 80%, and the
E
a
value drops significantly at this time, indicating that the excessive aging of the battery has begun to affect the electrode/electrolyte interface state. In addition, the SOH of samples 1–4 covers the range of 80%–100% of the battery, and their activation energy is basically stable at 0.55 eV, which indicates that the activation energy is not affected by the aging degree of the battery during normal use. However, when the battery life exceeds a critical value, the activation energy will drop rapidly, and the battery will face a serious risk of thermal runaway. Especially during charging, with the rapid decrease of activation energy, lithium-ions will more easily penetrate the SEI to reach the graphite electrode. The rapid transport of lithium-ions can lead to electrolyte decomposition at the electrode, which can generate a large amount of thermal accumulation at the anode, leading to thermal runaway [6,32]. By measuring the activation energy, the risk of thermal runaway can be predicted in advance. In summary, the activation energy obtained by DCIS can directly diagnose the state of charge transport at the electrode/electrolyte interface and improve the battery safety management capability.

In this paper, a method to detect the stability of the charge transport process in Li-ion batteries is presented. This method uses DCIS to detect the charge transport resistance at different temperatures, and then obtains the activation energy of the electrode/electrolyte interface according to the Arrhenius equation. The activation energy value is used as an indicator of the stability of the charge transport process. The experimental results show that when the SOH of the battery sample is greater than 80%, the activation energy of the battery remains stable at around 0.55 eV, and when the SOH of the sample battery is lower than 80%, the activation energy of the battery rapidly decreases to around 0.16 eV. The method of obtaining activation energy by DCIS can effectively detect the stability of the charge transport process, and can be used as a method for battery safety diagnosis.

The advantages of this method are as follows: (1) DCIS is a time-domain impedance spectroscopy technique. It uses a DC pulse with variable pulse width as a trigger signal, and directly uses the time function of the equivalent resistance to obtain the charge transfer resistance
R
c t
. Compared with the EIS technology, it is easier to implement in the battery management system and complete the online detection. (2) The activation energy is used as the risk indicator of thermal runaway of the battery, which effectively avoids the influence of temperature and aging on the diagnosis. This method is beneficial to detect batteries working in complex environments, which is of great significance for online safety detection of electric vehicles.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Shanghai Municipal Natural Science, China Fund under Grant No. 19ZR1402200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Questions and answers relating to lithium-ion battery safety issues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wensheng Huang, Xuning Feng, Xuebing Han, Weifeng Zhang, and Fachao Jiang. Questions and Answers Relating to Lithium-Ion Battery Safety Issues. Cell Reports Physical Science 2 (1) (2021) 1-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of lithium-ion battery safety concerns: The issues, strategies, and testing standards

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuqing Chen, Yuqiong Kang, Yun Zhao, Li Wang, Jilei Liu, Yanxi Li, Zheng Liang, Xiangming He, Xing Li, Naser Tavajohi, Baohua Li. A review of lithium-ion battery safety concerns: The issues, strategies, and testing standards. Journal of Energy Chemistry 59 (2021) 83-99.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A study of the overcharge reaction of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Randolph A. Leising, Marcus J. Palazzo, Esther Sans Takeuchi, Kenneth J. Takeuchi. A study of the overcharge reaction of lithium-ion batteries. Power Sources 97 (2001) 681-683.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigating lithium-ion battery materials during overcharge-induced thermal runaway: an operando and multi-scale X-ray CT study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Donal P. Finegan, Mario Scheel, James B. Robinson, Bernhard Tjaden, aMarco Di Michiel, Gareth Hinds, Dan J. L. Bretta and Paul R. Shearing. Investigating lithium-ion battery materials during overcharge-induced thermal runaway: an operando and multi-scale X-ray CT study. Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics 18 (45) (2016) 30912-30919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ solid state Li-7 NMR observations of lithium metal deposition during overcharge in lithium ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Arai, Y. Okada, T. Sugiyama, M. Izuka, K. Gotoh, K. akeda. In Situ Solid State Li-7 NMR Observations of Lithium Metal Deposition during Overcharge in Lithium Ion Batteries. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 162 (6) (2015) A952-A958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical-thermal coupled overcharge-to-thermal-runaway model for lithium ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dongsheng Ren, Xuning Feng, Languang Lu, Minggao Ouyang, Siqi Zheng, Jianqiu Li, Xiangming He. An electrochemical-thermal coupled overcharge-to-thermal-runaway model for lithium ion battery. Journal of Power Sources 364 (2017) 328-340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Charge transporting layer for high-rate and durable Li4Ti5O12-based Li-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Firoz Khana, Misol Ohb, Jae Hyun Kimb. N-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Charge transporting layer for high-rate and durable Li4Ti5O12-based Li-ion battery. Chemical Engineering Journal 369 (2019)1024-1033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacial effects in lithium and sodium batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Robert Usiskin and Joachim Maier. Interfacial Effects in Lithium and Sodium Batteries. Advanced Energy Materials 11 (2) (2021) 0

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy as a diagnostic tool for passive direct methanol fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.A. Braz, C.S. Moreira, V.B. Oliveira, A.M.F.R. Pinto. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy as a diagnostic tool for passive direct methanol fuel cells. Energy Reports 8 (2022) 7964-7975.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterisation of batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Laurence A. Middlemiss, Anthony J.R. Rennie, Ruth Sayers, Anthony R. West. Characterisation of batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Energy Reports 6 (5) (2020) 232-241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-health of li-ion battery estimation based on the efficiency of the charge transfer extracted from impedance spectra

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ahmed Yahia Kallel, Viktor Petrychenko and Olfa Kanoun. State-of-Health of Li-Ion Battery Estimation Based on the Efficiency of the Charge Transfer Extracted from Impedance Spectra. Applied Sciences-Basel 12 (2) (2022) 0

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to commercial Li-ion cells: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Meddings, N., Heinrich, M., Overney, F., Lee, JS., Ruiz, V., Napolitano, E., Seitz, S., Hinds, G., Raccichini, R., Gabers, M., Park, Juyeon. Application of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to commercial Li-ion cells: A review. Journal of Power Sources 480 (2020) 0

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Factors limiting li+ charge transfer kinetics in li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Richard Jow, Samuel A. Delp, Jan L. Allen, John-Paul Jones and Marshall C. Smart. Factors Limiting Li+ Charge Transfer Kinetics in Li-Ion Batteries. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 165 (2) (2018) A361-A367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature dependent EIS studies separating charge transfer impedance from contact impedance in lithium-ion symmetric cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. S. Keefe, Samuel Buteau, I. G. Hill, and J. R. Dahn. Temperature Dependent EIS Studies Separating Charge Transfer Impedance from Contact Impedance in Lithium-Ion Symmetric Cells. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 166 (14) (2019) A3272-A3279.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring SEI formation on graphite electrodes in lithium-ion cells by impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sophie Solchenbach, Xinyi Huang, Daniel Pritzl, Johannes Landesfeind, and Hubert A. Gasteiger. Monitoring SEI Formation on Graphite Electrodes in Lithium-Ion Cells by Impedance Spectroscopy. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 168 (2021) 0

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on electrochemical impedance spectrum: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dezhi Li, Licheng Wang, Chongxiong Duan, Qiang Li, and Kai Wang. Temperature prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on electrochemical impedance spectrum: A review. International Journal of Energy Research 0 (2022) 10372-10388. (2021)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Making li-metal electrodes rechargeable by controlling the dendrite growth direction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, Q. Liu, L. Xin, Y. Liu, F. Yang, E.A. Stach, J. Xie. Making Li-metal electrodes rechargeable by controlling the dendrite growth direction. Nature Energy 2 (7) (2017) 0

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Bieker, M. Winter, P. Bieker. Electrochemical in situ investigations of SEI and dendrite formation on the lithium metal anode. Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics 17 (14) (2015) 8670-8679.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The interplay between solid electrolyte interface (SEI) and dendritic lithium growth

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wu, J. Lochala, T. Taverne, J. Xiao. The interplay between solid electrolyte interface (SEI) and dendritic lithium growth. Nano Energy 40 (2017) 34-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on electric vehicle battery modelling: From lithium-iontoward lithium–sulphur

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abbas Fotouhi, Daniel J. Auger, Karsten Propp, Stefano Longo, Mark Wild. A review on electric vehicle battery modelling: From Lithium-iontoward Lithium–Sulphur. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews 56 (2016) 1008-1021. (2015)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterisation of batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Laurence A. Middlemiss, Anthony J.R. Rennie, Ruth Sayers, Anthony R. West. Characterisation of batteries by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Energy Reports 6 (2020) 232-241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of the differential capacity of lithium-ion batteries by the deconvolution of electrochemical impedance spectra

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Guo, G. Yang, G. Zhao, M. Yi, X. Feng, X. Han, L. Lu, M. Ouyang. Determination of the Differential Capacity of Lithium-Ion Batteries by the Deconvolution of Electrochemical Impedance Spectra. Energies 13 (4) (2020) 0.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid model-free state of health estimation for end-of-first-life electric vehicle batteries using impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Rastegarpanah, J. Hathaway, R. Stolkin. Rapid Model-Free State of Health Estimation for End-of-First-Life Electric Vehicle Batteries Using Impedance Spectroscopy. Energies 14 (9) (2021) 0

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Farmann, D.U. Sauer. Comparative study of reduced order equivalent circuit models for on-board state-of-available-power prediction of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. Applied Energy 255 (2018) 1102-1122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study of alithium/sulfur battery: Modeling and analysis of capacity fading

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Deng, Z. Zhang, Y. Lai, J. Liu, J. Li, Y. Liu. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study of alithium/sulfur battery: Modeling and analysis of capacity fading. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 160 (2013) A553–A558.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of the charging protocol on the cycle life of a Li-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Zhang. The effect of the charging protocol on the cycle life of a Li-ion battery. Journal of Power Sources 161 (2) (2006) 1385-1391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery real-time diagnosis with direct current impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Bao and Y. Chen. Lithium-Ion Battery Real-Time Diagnosis with Direct Current Impedance Spectroscopy. Energies 14 (15) (2021) 0

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pooja Vadhva, Ji Hu, Michael J. Johnson, Richard Stocker, Michele Braglia, Dan J. L. Brett, and Alexander J. E. Rettie. Theoretical and Experimental Strategies for New Heterostructures with Improved Stability for Rechargeable Lithium Sulfur Batteries. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 167 (4) (2020) 0

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Michael Theiler, Christian Endisch and Meinert Lewerenz. Float Current Analysis for Fast Calendar Aging Assessment of 18650 Li(NiCoAl)O2/Graphite Cells. Batteries 7 (2021) 22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature dependence of capacity and impedance data from fresh and aged high-power lithium-ion cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. P. Abraham, E. M. Reynolds, P. L. Schultz, A. N. Jansen, and D. W. Dees. Temperature dependence of capacity and impedance data from fresh and aged high-power lithium-ion cells. Journal of the Electrochemical Society 153 (8) (2006) A1610-A1616.

Takahisa Ohsaki, Takashi Kishi, Takashi Kuboki, Norio Takami, Nao Shimura, Yuichi Sato, Masahiro Sekino, Asako Satoh. Overcharge reaction of lithium-ion batteries. Journal of Power Sources 146 (1) (2005) 97-100.
